history.text: The brewery Föroya Bjór in Klaksvík is a Faroese family brewery, founded in 1888.

history.label: 20th century
history.text: The first settlement at Klaksvík dates back to Viking times, but it was not until the 20th century that the district merged to form a large, modern Faroese town that became a cultural and.
In time, they grew into four villages: Vágur, Myrkjanoyri, Gerðar and Uppsalir; which finally merged to form the town of Klaksvík in 1938.
Klaksvík is home to Summarfestivalurin. Notable buildings Christianskirkjan, built in 1963, is the first one in modern times in Scandinavia to be built in Norse style.
With the opening of the Leirvík sub-sea tunnel, the Norðoyatunnilin in April 2006, Klaksvík gained a physical link with the mainland of the Faroe Islands and can now be considered one of its key.
Since August 2007, when Restorffs Bryggjarí went out of business, Föroya Bjór has been the only producer of beer and soft drinks in the Faroe Islands.

history.label: Modern city
history.text: It has an important harbour with fishing industry and a modern fishing fleet.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: What triggered the development of the town was the establishment of a centralized store for all the northern islands on the location.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Several developments are under way to exploit this symbiosis, including a new industrial park located by the tunnel entrance.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: The roof construction is the same as that found in the Viking halls, and which has survived in Faroese smoke rooms (kitchens) and village churches.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: This open roof construction has proved to be especially suitable for church buildings, as the acoustics in this church are better than in others of a similar size.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: The church is dedicated to the memory of the sailors who lost their lives during World War II.
